summary: Removed the ftCommon-local type aliases `ftCo_GObj` and `ftCo_Fighter` and mechanically updated ftCommon/fighter code to use the canonical `Fighter_GObj` and `Fighter` types directly. The diff is broad but mostly type-signature churn across 238 files, with nearly balanced additions/deletions and no review/body text indicating behavior changes.
what_changed: Deleted `typedef Fighter ftCo_Fighter;` and `typedef Fighter_GObj ftCo_GObj;` from `src/melee/ft/chara/ftCommon/forward.h`.
Replaced function parameters, return types, locals, and prototypes using `ftCo_GObj*` with `Fighter_GObj*` throughout ftCommon source and headers.
Replaced function parameters, return types, locals, and prototypes using `ftCo_Fighter*` with `Fighter*` throughout ftCommon and related fighter headers.
Updated documentation/header surfaces such as `ftcommon.h`, `ftcommon.dox`, and large fighter headers so public declarations no longer expose the ftCo-specific aliases.
Touched many common-action files including guard, damage, escape, item throw, attack, cargo, hammer, thrown/capture, cliff, jump, pass, and other ftCommon state modules.
smart_moves: Removed duplicate local aliases instead of preserving two names for the same core fighter concepts, reducing type-name ambiguity across the fighter subsystem.
Used the existing canonical project types `Fighter` and `Fighter_GObj`, which makes signatures consistent with non-ftCommon fighter code.

Kept the change mechanical and type-only in the excerpt: examples show direct substitutions such as `ftCo_8009C4F8(ftCo_GObj* gobj)` to `ftCo_8009C4F8(Fighter_GObj* gobj)` and `ftCo_800A0384(ftCo_Fighter* fp)` to `ftCo_800A0384(Fighter* fp)`.
Propagated the alias removal through both `.c` definitions and `.h` declarations, avoiding mismatched prototypes after deleting the typedefs.
decomp_lessons: Avoid subsystem-prefixed typedef aliases for globally meaningful structs when they do not add semantic information; they increase search and prototype fragmentation.
When deleting a typedef from a forward header, update every declaration and definition in the same PR so downstream includes continue to compile cleanly.
Canonical type names improve grepability: future decomp work should prefer `Fighter*` and `Fighter_GObj*` for fighter data and fighter game objects instead of per-module aliases.

Large alias-removal PRs can be low-risk if they are strictly mechanical and have balanced add/delete counts, but reviewers and future agents should still verify no non-type logic was accidentally changed.

naming_conventions.lesson: Use the canonical fighter struct name rather than an ftCommon-specific alias.
Use the canonical fighter game-object typedef rather than an ftCommon-specific alias.
This PR only changed parameter/return/local type spelling; it did not rename ftCommon functions.

assembly_or_matching_tactics: Type-alias removal should not affect generated code when the underlying type is unchanged; this kind of cleanup is suitable when matching depends on preserving logic and layout while improving declarations.
For decomp cleanup, prefer direct canonical typedef substitution over introducing wrapper casts or temporary compatibility aliases.
Update headers and source in lockstep to preserve prototype consistency and avoid compiler-driven codegen differences from accidental implicit declarations.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or review bodies were present in the provided PR slice.
The PR was merged quickly on 2025-08-02, but the slice does not provide reviewer rationale.
follow_up_queries: Find remaining subsystem-local aliases of `Fighter` or `Fighter_GObj` in fighter headers.
Search for any remaining `ftCo_Fighter` or `ftCo_GObj` references after PR 1782.
Check whether other character-common modules define redundant aliases for canonical engine structs.
Audit future ftCommon declarations to ensure new prototypes use `Fighter*` and `Fighter_GObj*` directly.
